speaker: interviewer
text: Date is February 25th, and we are currently interviewing the rescue and aid-giver Renee Scott. Miss Scott, tell me about your liberation from Ravensbruck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, we were getting towards the end and we felt it. We knew everything was getting, you know, out of order. No more order. We didn't have to go to work all the time as we did before. Uh, the appell at 2 o'clock in the morning was not given any more. Uh, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The, uh, guards were coming to the camp drunk. They'd drink, come to the guard. They didn't know what they were saying. They didn't know how they were counting us. Nothing. So everything was going-- really breaking up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, two or three days after that, they came in the blocks and they said that they would come later on and call the numbers of people that had to go up front. We call that the Champs-Elysees of the camp. And there was a [INAUDIBLE], a big square. And when we were called, we went up there-- Lillian and I and some others, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were all desks up there with an officer and all, papers on all the desks. There were about 20 desks there. And then they called your number, and you had to go to the desk. And they asked you what nationality you were, and where you were going to. You're going to Belgium or to France or whatever. They took all that, the information they wanted, and then you go back on the lines-- standing up in lines. And they kept us like that a whole day long. That was bad. A whole day long we had to stay there in line. And that was really cruel, because they-- on purpose. They took us all around the camp, walking all around the camp when they had finished with all their paper stuff. And they stopped us in front of the Gestapo cause a-- we were kind of afraid anyway. We said-- trying to make believe they're going to be nice, but we have no-- no faith in them.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? So anyway, they stopped us in front of the lake, and the crematorium was right in front of the lake. So we said, gee, maybe they're going to stick us here in the crematorium alive. They did-- they did that with others. We weren't surprised, you know? So Lil said, listen, if they-- if you see they open that, we're going to jump in the lake. You stay with me. Even if you can't swim, it's better to die in the lake than to die in there, in that oven. So we stayed there. And we stayed there for hours and hours. And they kept opening up that, the oven and making it work, and closing it on purpose to frighten us, you know? So anyway, around-- I would say maybe 4 o'clock-- it was late, 4 o'clock in the afternoon. Here we started to move a little bit. We were so glad to get away from that cremator-- crematorium. So we started to walk and walk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then along the lake, there were all the officers' homes were there. Beautiful homes. So they walked us near the lake in front of their homes there. And from there, there was a little road that went up to Furstenberg, to the village of Furstenberg. That's the only nice-- the only nice thing we had when we were being counted. When it was a nice day, we could hear the bells of the church. So they brought us up there, and we could-- we had to stand there a long time. And we saw this road. We could see far, far, far. And after, it must have been about 7 o'clock at night. It was still very light. We saw coming down the road, great big trucks, great-- about four of them coming down that road.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we thought, my goodness, what's that? It was the Count Bernadotte. Were we happy. We were-- oh, we were crazy. We were jumping. And there was-- in front of the cars there was a-- a-- like an ambulance, a big ambulance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was in the first car, in a white car. And we wouldn't let him get by. We wouldn't let him get by. We were jumping in front of the car. He said, it's all right. Don't worry. He spoke English. Don't worry, he said. I'm here now. You're OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that-- oh, her-- her name was Bines. You want to know the- the- the mistress of-- her name-- you want to write it. B-I-N-E-S, Bines. She was there, and she was still hitting us with that bat. He was looking at her, and he couldn't tell her to stop. But she was still hitting us, because she saw we were so happy, you know? Anyway, he said, I'm not going to be long, but I have to go. So he went into the camp to talk to the commander of the camp-- that Bines' boyfriend. Anyway, he went in there, and he stayed for a while. And apparently-- now how true this is-- this is what we were told. Don't tell me-- don't tell me it's not true or it is true. I don't know how true it is. Apparently, he made-- he-- how do you say negotiate-- negotiated? Is that correct? Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he bought us for gold and for flour. He gave the Germans flour and gold to get 165 of us. He bought 165 that day. That was a first transport. I was in that one. So he-- when they-- when he came back, we wouldn't let him get by either. We said, you can't go, you can't go. He said, but you're coming too, you're coming too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all those, uh, military trucks had a big-- you know, but there was no door. And they had like a shade that comes down. I can't explain that. And so Lil and I were always together, so we took one of the last ones.

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. So we got into those trucks. And apparently, the Red Cross had Red Cross packages, and they were giving them out to us. I don't know why I think I was one of the shortest where I didn't get any. But I didn't care. I said I don't care. I want to get in the truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we get in the truck, and there were two ambulances that followed. Some- some prisoners were too sick to get in the trucks. They put them in the ambulance. And we started to get off. And all these trucks were-- were driven by volunteer, American volunteers, boys that were soldiers over there and were volunteered to take us away. So when we started to go, it was getting dark. About 8 o'clock, probably, when we started to go. And we went and we went, and we were all so tired that we just fell on one another and just fell asleep. And all at once, we woke up. Lillian and I, we heard very, very, very, very quietly-- we heard that-- that thing going up to let air come in. And then we heard what somebody said, they're all right, they're all right. So Lillian looked at me, and she said, did I hear something? I said, yes, I heard 'they're all right.' So we looked at the boy. He was an American boy. I don't know where he was from.

speaker: interviewee
text: We said, you speak English. He said, yes, I'm American. Oh, we said, gee, we speak English, too. So he took us. There were two, and they took us in their arms like that, and they put us down on the ground. And they said, now you keep quiet. Don't say anything. We said, we're so hungry. We're so thirsty. We're going to give you something. So they put a blanket down on the ground. But we were running away, and the Germans were spying on top. The planes were. So he said, no noise, and don't talk until we tell you to talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he put the blanket down. He said, would you like some tea? Oh, tea, what's tea? So they had those little military things that you can't see. I don't know how they do it, but anyway. They had military, uh, uh, stove, or whatever. I don't know how they do it. With batteries, probably. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, he may us tea, and he gave us something to eat. Oh my goodness, we didn't know what-- how to thank him. So he stayed with us, then he made us get back to the truck. And that same day-- yeah, that night-- they were bombing right near us. We were right near. So the commander of the convoy said, no we can't go yet. We have to stay.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed there. And the next morning, early, we started to go for Lubeck. That's at the border of Denmark, almost Denmark. It's Germany, still, but it's one of the last. You know where Lubeck is, probably. Anyway, we're going to Lubeck. And before Lubeck, we were shelled by the Russians. They said they made a mistake, that they thought that it was German convoys, because the Germans put crosses on all their, you know, their convoys. And the Germans were getting in between our cars. They were coming, too. They were going to Lubeck, towards Lubeck. They were following us, but they didn't dare do anything to us, see? So the Russians said they made a mistake. Anyway, we lost 85 prisoners on the road. There were heads here, the arms there. It was awful. And the little-- the driver-- in Germany there are a lot of-- I don't know now, but there were what they call saucer roads. The roads were, you know, a road like that, and then big ditches like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he had the [NON-ENGLISH]. He thought, I'm going to dip them in. And he dipped us in that ditch. That way, the plane went on top of us. It didn't hit us. And we got out of that plane as if nothing was-- you would have thought we were a bunch of chickens running away. Where we found our strength, I don't know. But we got out of there. And when the Red Cross came to look-- they were with us, you know, following us. They come to look for us. We were sitting there opening up the packages to eat. We didn't think of anything. Then he said-- told us he had sad news to tell us, that we had lost a lot of prisoners.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then we had to get back in the trucks, and getting to Lubeck. When we get to Lubeck, the German people-- but apparently they didn't know what to do to try to make us a little more comfortable. Some had made soup, and some had given-- wanted to give food and all kinds of things. So we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us-- the Red Cross said we could accept it, you know. It had been probably tested. I don't know, but anyway. So we ate a little bit there, and then, uh, from there, I think they transferred us to trains. But in Lubeck, right near where the buses-- the convoy came. And they put us in sanitary trains with a nurse and a doctor-- the ones that could sit up OK. Those that were too tired had to lay down. And we were normally going towards Denmark. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They want to take us to Copenhagen. And we got-- I think we were about-- I don't know-- maybe 50 miles away from Lubeck, and they started again shelling.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they stopped the train. They made us get down. We had to hide again in the ditches and, you know, on the side. Then we got back on the train, and I think we didn't go too, too far with the train. They put us back again in buses-- in a bus, I think, like a sanitary bus. And there we went-- that was one night, two nights. That must have been the third-- that happened in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at night, then. That was the next morning then, and we went maybe to noontime. Then they put us back again in a train. Got back in the train, and there we went to Padborg. We were at the Danish border. And Padborg is the first city that we met, and we got off in Denmark. And when we got there, the music was waiting for us, big band. The mayor was there. Flowers were there. A huge, great big farm like they have in Denmark-- beautiful farm-- they had transferred that farm into a village for us. There were doctors. There were nurses. There was a restaurant, with everything, everything for us. And they wanted us first to get all washed up. They'd made washrooms and everything. We had to get washed up with the help of a nurse. And they gave us something clean to put on like an overall, clean overall. And then we had to go and rest. They wanted us to rest at least one hour. So we went and rested in, uh, folding beds. Folding beds, all clean, all made of paper. Everything is paper in Sweden and Denmark. Everything is paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we rested. And then, when we got up, they told-- with the music-- they got us up. And the mayor and diplomats from the city and other cities came and spoke and all that. And they had great big, long tables set-- beautifully set-- flowers on the table and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they served us all a dinner like we hadn't seen in three and a half years, I'm telling you. So we had dinner, and then they made us rest again a little bit. Then they put us back in trains. We were a little bit cleaner-- not clean, but cleaner, you know. We got back in the trains. And there we were going to travel in that train to Copenhagen. It was quite far. And the Red Cross came and spoke to Lillian and I because we spoke English. And he said, the Gestapo is on the train, but don't worry, because they will come and they're going to try to frighten you. But ignore them. We're here. Ignore them. But we were afraid anyway, because they had revolvers and, you know, they didn't care what they did. So anyway, we continued to go. And effectively, one of them came and he said to Lillian and I-- he said in German-- he said, you think you're going to get home, but you're not going to go home. You're not going to get that far. Anyway, we made believe we didn't understand what he said.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we continued to go, and we got to Copenhagen. Then in Copenhagen, they got us off the train and they were going to put us in a boat. So we got to a boat, and that boat was turned into like a hospital, like a hospital boat. And there, a doctor-- we got two doctors.

speaker: interviewee
text: As we got on the boat, he just looked at us a little bit, asked if we had anything, you know, special that we wanted to be taken care of. We were too nervous. We wanted to get on the boat. We didn't care. We got on the boat. That's what we called our dirty boat, because we were still dirty. So we got on the dirty boat, and they served us something to eat again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had something to eat, and then-- uh, I think it was about early evening-- they got-- maybe I'm mixed up with my time. Anyway, early evening-- it was still light. They told us that we were going to change boat. But before changing boat, we were going to go in buses. And we were going to go to a school in the city, in Copenhagen. And we thought, school? Why a school?

speaker: interviewee
text: But that school was all turned into a department store. And on one side, it was like a hospital. Doctors were there, all separate rooms. Nurses were there. And you had to go see the doctor, and he looked at your ears, your eyes, your throat, everything, to see if anything special. Then you went to the nurse, and she'd take other tests. Then you went into a washroom. And a nurse was there, and she'd give you a bath. Each one had a bath. And then you'd get out of there, and you go into the department store. They gave you like a, uh, like a kimono, like a little kimono to put on you. And you'd go into the department store. And everything was marked, the sizes. There you had stockings. There you had shoes. There you have panties, underwear-- all kinds of underwear. Then further-- everybody had the same thing. Little Dutch overalls with little Dutch wooden shoes. It was cute. Little red ones and little blue ones. So we were all dressed like Dutch. Everybody was Dutch.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we got out of there-- now they said, now you're going to go in a clean bus, and you're going to go all around the city. So we got on buses, and the people didn't know what to do. They were all there. Their arms full of cakes and candies and everything they could give us. Then when we get through with that, they took us down, back to the clean boat-- going on a clean boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there we were going to Malmo on the boat. And that's where that German, that Gestapo, had said, you're not going to get home. Because he said that there would be a bomb that would blow up the boat. So anyway, we got on the boat, and we got to Malmo very nicely. About-- took about two hours-- two and a half hours, I think, from Copenhagen. Then when we got to Malmo, we were taken to the Museum of Malmo. And that's where Lillian and I were interpreters, at the Malmo museum. And then when we got there, we stayed there for, I think, about a week, a week or 10 days. And there, they select-- they put selections of where we would go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were sent to Pytterud. That's about 90 miles away from Stockholm in the country, way out in the country. So we went there, and-- after the week we stayed at the museum, we went there. And we stayed there until we got home. I think when we got home probably in May or something like that when we got back to Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother-in-law, Yvonne's husband-- at that time, he was then-- he wasn't at the Guaranty Trust anymore. He was director of the BB-- the British and French Bank in London. But he had come back to France, and her too, to see me, to meet me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, when we got to Malmo, stayed there. Then we went to Almhult, and he got in touch. A Swedish officer got in touch with me, and he asked me where my family was. I told him, and he cabled to Paul, my brother-in-law. And my brother-in-law sent a big check. And he said, now you offer a great big dinners to all your companions that are there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lived in a little wooden school in Sweden. We stayed at that little wooden school all the time we were there. And there was everything there. You could cook. You could do anything you want. The nurse was the minister's wife, so she went and did all the shopping. And we had a great big dinner. We had a good time, I'm telling you. So that's the way we stayed in Malmo-- uh, Pytterud, I mean. And then from Pytterud, the day we had to leave to come back, everybody was crying. The whole village was crying. We were crying. We didn't want to leave them anymore. They were so good to us. That's why I said Sweden is my second country. They saved me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first come in contact with John after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: With your, uh, your fiance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, he got in touch with my brother-in-law. And my-- my-- my John told my brother-in-law that I was saved. He got to know it first, because he was also director of the International Red Cross in Belgium. So he got to know it first. So he told my brother-in-law. They were very friendly. So that's the way it really finished. Happy day finally came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you eventually were reunited with your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were eventually reunited with your family in Paris--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Paris, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told us earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. First with John in Brussels, and then, uh, from there, I went to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after you were-- what happened after you were really reunited with your family in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I had to rest and see doctors, you know, doctors visits. Have a very quiet life until the doctor said, now I think she needs a change. So we went and lived in the south of France. We went near Nice. John took care of that. He said, well, we're going to find a place where you can stay, where she'll be well taken care of with the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he rented a great big place right near Nice, 12 miles from Nice along the Riviera. We had a good time. Alice, my daughter, was there, too. Everybody came, the whole family. We stayed there. We had that place for about three years, over three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Beautiful place. It was called the villa of-- the villa-- villa is a home-- of the violets. It was loaded with violets everywhere. And we had-- we had artichokes, and we had figs. You walked on the figs. We had big things of figs at the table all the time, fresh figs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just rested. Nothing. Going bathing, rested, nothing. I needed a rest, I'm telling you.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with your mother and father as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Father died in '40, 1940. My mother was there-- Mother. And the family would come from Paris to visit and stay the time they wanted. There was room for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you do after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went back-- well, when we went back to Paris. We left, finally, Nice, because my daughter was getting bigger. She wanted to find a job. And we went back, and we had our apartment in Paris. Pardon me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went back to Paris. What did I do? I didn't do much of anything. I didn't really work. I worked in the fairs. And then, John was still there. I was working for him as secretary. He traveled a lot with the fairs, so I'd go with him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Traveled a lot, worked, you know, with him. And when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well my daughter married a GI. Unfortunately, a bad GI. She was very miserable. She went through everything bad with him. She had three children. Two were born-- two girls born in Paris, and Marc was on his way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told her-- I said, you can stay here with me in Brussels if you want to. Things were not going good, you know. So I said, if you want to stay here, you're welcome. But she said, well, if I go to the States, maybe I'll try. Maybe it'll be better. She came here with the two girls and expecting-- Marc was almost born on the ship. I think he was born a few days after she arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were so cruel to him. Alice had real long black hair, way down. Her mother-in-law took her by the hair. She pulled out her hair so much she had a great big bald spot. Her hair was gone. She pulled her hair right out of her head. She was so mean to her, and the husband wasn't good either. He didn't care if the children had food. He didn't care about anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your two granddaughters' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What are your two granddaughters' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Patricia and Carole, with an E. And Marc, with a C. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you eventually followed your daughter to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not right away. I-- I wanted to see what was going to go on. She wrote letters from the police station here in-- right here, right up here. It used to be up here. She went there. She had to go to the police. They were beating her up all the time. They wouldn't even buy medicine for the baby. The baby was born at the New England Hospital. And, uh, she said she couldn't take it anymore. She was writing me letters, sad letters, and I was so worried. I only had one daughter, and my mother, in between, had died. So I was terribly upset. I said I'm going to sell everything, and I'm going to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I sold everything. I had everything new. Everything new, because the Gestapo had taken everything. So I had everything new in my beautiful apartment. I just sold everything. Lillian helped me. She helped to sell things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came here to find my poor daughter living down at that horrible Bromley-Heath thing there. That's where she was living. I got her out of there as quick as I could. I was lucky to find a job right-- almost right away. I found a job as hostess in a French restaurant. It was better than nothing. And I was very happy. And they were my first friends here in Boston.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've lived here ever since?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've lived in Boston ever since.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, right here in Jamaica Plain. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand you have some beautiful great-grandchildren as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have three beautiful great-grandchildren. I have my great-- my grandchildren. I have Marc, I have Patricia, and I have Carole. And I have three great-grand. I have Jennifer O'Halloran, I have Danielle O'Halloran, and now I have Curt Lieber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Congratulations.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Scott, we're going to change tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to change tapes now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
